# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the continuity equation for a steady, incompressible, one-dimensional fluid flow. Explain its physical significance in the design of flow measurement transducers.**

**Model Answer:**

> The continuity equation for a steady, incompressible, one-dimensional flow is:
>
> $$ A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q $$
>
> where $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $V$ is the average velocity, and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (m³/s). The subscript 1 and 2 denote two different sections of the flow.

**Physical Significance in Flow Transducers:**

- It establishes the **velocity–area relationship** ($V \propto 1/A$) — when area is reduced, velocity increases.
- It is the **foundation for the discharge equation** used in Venturi, orifice, and nozzle flowmeters. The relationship $Q = A_2 V_2$ is what links the *velocity* (deduced from Bernoulli's equation) to the *volumetric flow rate* the transmitter outputs.
- Without continuity, the calibration constants ($C_d$) of flowmeters cannot be uniquely defined.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Write Bernoulli's equation in head form. What are the assumptions made in deriving it?**

**Model Answer:**

Bernoulli's equation in head form (energy per unit weight of fluid):

$$ \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 $$

Where each term has units of **meters of fluid** (head).

**Assumptions:**

1. **Steady flow** — flow properties at any point do not change with time.
2. **Incompressible flow** — fluid density $\rho$ is constant.
3. **Inviscid flow** — viscosity (and hence friction) is neglected.
4. **Flow along a streamline** — the equation holds for a single streamline.
5. **No shaft work** — no pump or turbine between sections 1 and 2.

> For real flow, a head-loss term $h_L$ is added to account for viscous dissipation.

---

## 5.2 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always write the **starting form of the equation** before substituting values. Students lose **2 marks** for skipping this.
> - Show **unit conversion** explicitly. Mixing mm and m is the **#1 reason** students lose 1–2 marks.
> - The **discharge coefficient $C_d$** must be included in the final answer for flowmeter problems. Forgetting it costs **1 full mark**.
> - For momentum problems, draw a **clear free-body diagram** (or label forces in text) before writing the equation. Students lose 2 marks for omitting this.
> - Always state **assumptions** (steady, incompressible, inviscid) explicitly — 1 mark reserved for this in most questions.

---

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive Bernoulli's equation starting from Euler's equation of motion along a streamline. State all assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A horizontal Venturi meter with pipe diameter 150 mm and throat diameter 75 mm is used to measure the flow of water. A differential U-tube mercury manometer connected across the meter shows a deflection of 220 mm. Calculate: (i) the flow rate of water in litres/second, and (ii) the velocity of water at the throat. Take $C_d = 0.98$ and $S_{Hg} = 13.6$. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to A(a) — Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (7 Marks)**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady flow
2. Incompressible flow ($\rho$ = constant)
3. Inviscid flow (μ = 0, no friction)
4. Flow along a streamline
5. No external work (pumps/turbines)

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline.

**Step 1:** Net force along the streamline:

Pressure force on the rear face (acting forward) = $P \cdot dA$.

Pressure force on the front face (acting backward) = $(P + dP) \cdot dA$.

Net pressure force = $P \cdot dA - (P + dP) \cdot dA = -dP \cdot dA$.

Gravity component along the streamline:

$$
-dW = \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \cos\theta = \rho g \, dA \, dz \quad \text{(since } ds\cos\theta = dz\text{)}
$$

**Step 2:** Newton's Second Law:

$$
\text{Force} = \text{mass} \times \text{acceleration}
$$

$$
-dP \cdot dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = (\rho \, dA \, ds) \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $dA$:

$$
-dP - \rho g \, dz = \rho \, ds \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}
$$

**Step 4:** Use chain rule: $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$.

**Step 5:** Apply steady-flow assumption ($\partial V/\partial t = 0$): $\frac{dV}{dt} = V \frac{dV}{ds}$.

**Step 6:** Substitute:

$$
-dP - \rho g \, dz = \rho V \, dV
$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange and divide by $\rho$:

$$
\frac{dP}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0
$$

**Step 8:** Integrate from (1) to (2) with $\rho$ constant:

$$
\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{\rho} + \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV + \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz = 0
$$

**Step 9:** Evaluate integrals:

$$
\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1) = 0
$$

**Step 10:** Rearrange:

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2 \,}
$$

This is Bernoulli's equation — the energy equation for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.

---

#### **Solution to A(b) — Venturi Flow Rate Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 150$ mm = 0.150 m
- $D_2 = 75$ mm = 0.075 m
- $x = 220$ mm = 0.220 m (manometer deflection)
- $C_d = 0.98$
- $S_{Hg} = 13.6$

**Step 1 — Compute head difference in metres of water (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta h = x \left( \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) = 0.220 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.220 \times 12.6 = 2.772 \text{ m of water}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate cross-sectional areas (1 mark):**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^2 = 1.7671 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.4179 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute area ratio and velocity coefficient (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{4.4179 \times 10^{-3}}{1.7671 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.250
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9682
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\sqrt{2g\Delta h}$ (1 mark):**

$$
\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.772} = \sqrt{54.39} = 7.375 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate volumetric flow rate $Q$ (2 marks):**

$$
Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2g \Delta h}
$$

$$
Q = 0.98 \times \frac{4.4179 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9682} \times 7.375
$$

$$
Q = 0.98 \times 4.5631 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.375
$$

$$
Q = 3.297 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 32.97 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Velocity at the throat (1 mark):**

$$
V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{3.297 \times 10^{-2}}{4.4179 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.46 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{\, Q \approx 0.0330 \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 33.0 \text{ L/s} \,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, V_2 \approx 7.46 \text{ m/s} \,}
$$

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) State and explain the momentum equation for a steady flow of fluid through a control volume. A jet of water of diameter 50 mm strikes a stationary flat plate normally with a velocity of 20 m/s. Calculate the force exerted on the plate. (7 Marks)**

**(b) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Pitot-static tube for flow measurement. Derive the expression for the velocity of flow. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B(a) — Momentum Equation and Jet Force (7 Marks)**

**Statement (2 marks):**
Newton's second law for a fluid control volume in steady state states:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA
$$

For 1-D flow with one inlet (1) and one outlet (2), this reduces to:

$$
\boxed{\, \sum F_x = \rho Q (V_{2x} - V_{1x}) \,}
$$

The net external force on the control volume equals the net rate of momentum efflux.

**Given (calculation, 5 marks):**
- Jet diameter $d = 50$ mm = 0.050 m
- Jet velocity $V = 20$ m/s
- Strikes a flat plate **normally** (perpendicular)

**Step 1 — Calculate jet area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate volumetric flow rate $Q$:**

$$
Q = A \cdot V = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 = 3.927 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify velocities before and after impact:**

- Inlet velocity (jet hitting plate) $V_1 = 20$ m/s (along x-axis)
- Outlet velocity: the water spreads radially on the plate → $V_{2x} = 0$ (no momentum in x-direction leaves the CV)

**Step 4 — Apply momentum equation:**

$$
F = \rho Q (V_{2x} - V_{1x}) = 1000 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-2} \times (0 - 20)
$$

$$
F = -1000 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-2} \times 20 = -785.4 \text{ N}
$$

The negative sign indicates that the force on the fluid is in the negative x-direction (decelerating it). By Newton's third law, the force exerted **by the fluid on the plate** is equal and opposite:

$$
\boxed{\, F_{plate} = 785.4 \text{ N (in the direction of the jet)} \,}
$$

---

#### **Solution to B(b) — Pitot-Static Tube (7 Marks)**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

A Pitot-static tube has two openings:
1. A **stagnation (impact) opening** at the front, facing the flow → measures the **stagnation pressure** $P_0$ (also called total pressure or Pitot pressure).
2. **Static pressure tap(s)** on the side walls of the tube → measure the **static pressure** $P$ of the undisturbed flow.

The tube is inserted into the fluid stream. The differential pressure $\Delta P = P_0 - P$ is measured by a manometer or differential pressure transducer. Since the stagnation point brings the fluid to rest, applying Bernoulli's equation between a far-upstream point (1) and the stagnation point (2) directly gives the flow velocity.

**Sketch (described in text since this is an answer key, 1 mark):**

The tube has a bent, concentric design:
- An inner L-shaped tube open at the tip facing the flow.
- An outer tube with side holes on the cylindrical surface.
- The two pressures are routed to a differential manometer (or transducer).

**Derivation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between upstream point (1, where flow has velocity $V$ and static pressure $P$) and the stagnation point (2, where velocity = 0 and pressure = $P_0$):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $V_2 = 0$, $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal), $P_1 = P$, $P_2 = P_0$:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$
\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0 - P}{\rho g} = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V$:

$$
\boxed{\, V = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{2g \Delta h} \,}
$$

Where $\Delta h$ is the manometer head (in metres of flowing fluid) and $\Delta P$ is the differential pressure.

**Engineering Notes:**
- A correction factor $C_v$ (velocity coefficient, typically 0.98–0.99) is sometimes multiplied to account for non-idealities.
- Pitot-static tubes measure *point velocity*, not the bulk flow rate. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is then obtained by integrating the velocity profile across the pipe cross-section: $Q = \int V \, dA$.

---

## 5.3 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4**

### ✅ The Three Sacred Equations

- [ ] **Continuity:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ (incompressible) / $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ (general)
- [ ] **Momentum:** $\sum F = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$
- [ ] **Energy (Bernoulli):** $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$

### ✅ Master Formula (Most Asked in KTU)

- [ ] $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_t}{\sqrt{1-(A_t/A_p)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2g \Delta h}$

### ✅ Assumptions Checklist (Write These First in Every Derivation)

- [ ] Steady flow
- [ ] Incompressible (or note if compressible)
- [ ] Inviscid (for Bernoulli) — add $h_L$ for real flow
- [ ] Along a streamline
- [ ] No pump/turbine between sections

### ✅ Key Constants (No Excuse to Forget)

- [ ] $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- [ ] $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- [ ] $\gamma_{water} = 9810$ N/m³
- [ ] $S_{Hg} = 13.6$

### ✅ Manometer Equation (Always Tested)

- [ ] $\Delta h = x \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1 \right)$ where $x$ is the deflection, $\rho_m$ is the manometer fluid, $\rho_f$ is the flowing fluid

### ✅ Discharge Coefficient Values

- [ ] Venturi: $C_d \approx 0.95$ to $0.99$
- [ ] Nozzle: $C_d \approx 0.92$ to $0.98$
- [ ] Orifice: $C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.65$

### ✅ Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)

- [ ] ⚠️ Mixing units (mm with m) — **always convert to SI first**
- [ ] ⚠️ Forgetting $C_d$ in the final flow equation
- [ ] ⚠️ Using gauge pressure vs. absolute pressure inconsistently
- [ ] ⚠️ Omitting the "assumptions" section in derivations
- [ ] ⚠️ Confusing $V_{throat}$ with $V_{upstream}$ — they're different
- [ ] ⚠️ Skipping the free-body diagram in momentum problems
- [ ] ⚠️ Forgetting to convert manometer reading from cm/mm to metres
- [ ] ⚠️ Not stating incompressibility in Bernoulli's equation applications

### ✅ Mapping to Transducers (What Drives What?)

| Transducer | Primary Equations |
|------------|-------------------|
| **Venturi** | Bernoulli + Continuity → $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta h}$ |
| **Orifice** | Bernoulli + Continuity → $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta h}$ (with smaller $C_d$) |
| **Pitot tube** | Bernoulli only (between upstream & stagnation) |
| **Target meter** | Momentum → $F \propto \rho V^2 A$ |
| **Bend meter** | Momentum (vector) + Pressure forces |
| **Nozzle** | Bernoulli + Continuity (no long recovery cone) |

### ✅ Bloom's Taxonomy Coverage for This Module

- **L1 (Remember):** Definitions of continuity, momentum, energy equations
- **L2 (Understand):** Explanation of physical significance, assumptions
- **L3 (Apply):** Using Bernoulli to solve for $\Delta P$ or $V$ in given scenarios
- **L4 (Analyze):** Deriving the discharge equation for Venturi/orifice
- **L5 (Evaluate):** Comparing $C_d$ values and explaining why orifice has lower $C_d$ than Venturi
- **L6 (Create):** Designing a flow measurement system for a given application (advanced)

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Out of the 14-mark questions in this module, **80% are either "derive the discharge equation" or "calculate Q for a given Venturi/orifice/Pitot geometry."** Master the master formula in §2.4 and the worked example in §3.5, and you will secure at least **10–12 marks** on any question from this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bernoulli's Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Bernoulli's Equations ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the domain of **Transducers and Measurements**, specifically under fluid-flow measurement transducers, **Bernoulli's Equation** is the foundational principle derived from the **conservation of mechanical energy** for a steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow of a fluid along a streamline. Mathematically, it states that the sum of the pressure head, the velocity head (kinetic energy per unit weight), and the elevation head (potential energy per unit weight) remains constant between any two points along a streamline. For two points **1** and **2** along a streamline in an ideal fluid: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ Where: - $P$ = Static pressure of the fluid (Pa or N/m²) - $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³) - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**) - $v$ = Flow velocity of the fluid (m/s) - $z$ = Elevation above a chosen datum (m) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Examiner's Word Bank):** "Bernoulli's Equation is an energy conservation statement for an ideal fluid, expressing that the total mechanical energy (pressure + kinetic + potential) per unit weight of fluid remains constant along a streamline." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at the top of a water slide holding a ball. At the top, you have lots of **potential energy** (height), but the ball is barely moving (low kinetic energy), and the water pressure pushing on the ball is relatively low. As the slide narrows and drops downward, two things happen simultaneously: 1. **The ball speeds up** (more kinetic energy, more velocity head). 2. **The ball loses height** (less potential energy, lower elevation head). 3. **The water pressure drops** because the energy is being converted into motion rather than push. Bernoulli's equation says: *Whatever energy the ball loses in height or pressure, it gains in speed — and vice versa.* The **total** stays the same. In engineering terms, when a fluid passes through a **constriction** (a narrow region in a pipe), the fluid must speed up to maintain mass conservation. According to Bernoulli, this increase in velocity head must be paid for by a **decrease in pressure head**. This drop in pressure is exactly what flow-measuring instruments (Venturi meters, orifice plates, flow nozzles, Pitot tubes) exploit to determine flow rate. ### 1.3 Standard Constants and Notation > [!NOTE] > **Standardized Engineering Constants (per ISO 5167 / KTU Board):** > - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$ > - Density of water at 4 °C: $\rho_{water} = \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3}$ > - Density of air at STP: $\rho_{air} \approx \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ > - Specific weight of water: $\gamma_w = \rho g = \mathbf{9810 \; N/m^3}$ --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions (Strict Examination Focus) Bernoulli's Equation, in its pure form, is valid only when **all** the following assumptions hold. Examiners frequently test these: 1. **Steady Flow** — The flow parameters at any point do not change with time ($\partial v/\partial t = 0$, $\partial P/\partial t = 0$). 2. **Incompressible Fluid** — Density $\rho$ remains constant ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$). 3. **Inviscid (Frictionless) Flow** — Viscosity effects and frictional losses are negligible. 4. **Flow Along a Streamline** — The equation applies between two points lying on the same streamline. 5. **No Shaft Work / No Heat Transfer** — No energy is added or removed by pumps, turbines, or heat exchangers between the two points. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Trap:** If a real pipe has friction, a **head-loss term** $h_L$ must be added, transforming Bernoulli's Equation into the **Modified (or Extended) Energy Equation**: > $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ > Many students lose marks by forgetting this correction in real-system problems. ### 2.2 The Companion Equation — Continuity Equation Bernoulli's Equation alone cannot solve flow problems; it must be coupled with the **Continuity Equation** (mass conservation): $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q$$ For incompressible flow, this states that the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is conserved. If the cross-sectional area decreases, the velocity must increase proportionally. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) The following table consolidates every Bernoulli-derived equation a KTU student must memorize for Module 4: | **#** | **Formula** | **Description / Application** | **Units** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | Bernoulli's Equation (ideal fluid) | m (of fluid) | | 2 | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$ | Bernoulli's Equation (pressure form) | Pa | | 3 | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q$ | Continuity Equation (incompressible) | m³/s | | 4 | $v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | Velocity at throat / constriction | m/s | | 5 | $Q_{th} = A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | Theoretical volumetric flow rate | m³/s | | 6 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | Actual flow rate (Venturi / orifice) | m³/s | | 7 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}} \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Venturi / orifice in terms of **diameters** | m³/s | | 8 | $v = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Velocity from Pitot-static tube | m/s | | 9 | $h_L = \dfrac{f L v^2}{2 g D}$ | Darcy–Weisbach head loss (friction) | m | | 10 | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Coefficient of discharge (orifice) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation Key for the Board:** > - $h$ = Differential pressure head, expressed in **metres of the flowing fluid** $= (P_1 - P_2)/\rho g$ > - $C_d$ = Coefficient of discharge ($0.6$–$0.98$ typical) > - $C_c$ = Coefficient of contraction ($0.6$–$0.65$ typical) > - $C_v$ = Coefficient of velocity ($0.95$–$0.99$ typical) > - $D_1, D_2$ = Pipe diameter and throat diameter respectively ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Bernoulli principle underpins virtually every industrial flow measurement device: - **Venturi Meter** — Used in petrochemical pipelines, water treatment, and HVAC systems where minimum pressure loss is critical. - **Orifice Plate** — The most widely used flow meter globally, due to simplicity and low cost. - **Flow Nozzle** — Used for high-velocity steam flow measurement in power plants. - **Pitot-Static Tube** — Used in aircraft airspeed indicators and meteorological wind measurements. - **Rotameter / Float-type flowmeter** — Operates on the principle that a pressure drop across the float is balanced by its weight. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation **Setup:** Consider a fluid element of volume $dV$ flowing inside a pipe of varying cross-section between two sections **1** and **2** along a streamline. Let: - At section 1: pressure $P_1$, velocity $v_1$, area $A_1$, elevation $z_1$ - At section 2: pressure $P_2$, velocity $v_2$, area $A_2$, elevation $z_2$ - Density $\rho$ is constant (incompressible) **Step 1 — Forces acting on the fluid element due to pressure:** The pressure force on the upstream face (at section 1) acts in the direction of flow: $$F_1 = P_1 \cdot A_1 \quad (\text{acting in the direction of motion})$$ The pressure force on the downstream face (at section 2) acts opposite to the flow: $$F_2 = P_2 \cdot A_2 \quad (\text{acting against the direction of motion})$$ **Step 2 — Work done by pressure forces during a small time interval $dt$:** The fluid element moves a small distance $ds_1$ at section 1 and $ds_2$ at section 2 in time $dt$. $$\text{Work at section 1} = P_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot ds_1 = P_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot v_1 \cdot dt$$ $$\text{Work at section 2} = -P_2 \cdot A_2 \cdot ds_2 = -P_2 \cdot A_2 \cdot v_2 \cdot dt$$ Net work done by pressure forces: $$W_{net} = \left( P_1 A_1 v_1 - P_2 A_2 v_2 \right) \cdot dt$$ **Step 3 — Apply continuity equation to simplify:** By the continuity equation, $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q$ (volumetric flow rate). Therefore: $$W_{net} = \left( P_1 - P_2 \right) \cdot Q \cdot dt$$ **Step 4 — Change in Kinetic Energy of the fluid element:** The mass of the fluid element is $m = \rho \cdot Q \cdot dt$. Its kinetic energy change between section 1 and section 2 is: $$\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m \left( v_2^2 - v_1^2 \right) = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, Q \, dt \left( v_2^2 - v_1^2 \right)$$ **Step 5 — Change in Potential Energy of the fluid element:** As the fluid moves from elevation $z_1$ to elevation $z_2$: $$\Delta PE = m g (z_2 - z_1) = \rho \, Q \, dt \, g \, (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 6 — Apply the Work–Energy Theorem:** The net work done by all forces equals the change in total mechanical energy: $$W_{net} = \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$ Substituting all the expressions: $$\left( P_1 - P_2 \right) Q \, dt = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, Q \, dt \left( v_2^2 - v_1^2 \right) + \rho \, Q \, dt \, g \, (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 7 — Cancel the common factor $Q \, dt$ from both sides:** $$P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \left( v_2^2 - v_1^2 \right) + \rho g (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 8 — Rearrange into the standard Bernoulli form:** $$\boxed{\; \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 \;}$$ This is the classical Bernoulli's Equation. Each term has units of **metres** (of fluid head). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Discharge Equation for a Constriction-Type Flowmeter (Venturi / Orifice) This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 4. It transforms Bernoulli's Equation into a usable flow-rate formula. **Setup:** A horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$) contains a constriction. Two pressure tappings measure the upstream pressure $P_1$ (in the main pipe of area $A_1$) and the throat pressure $P_2$ (in the constriction of area $A_2$). **Step 1 — Simplify Bernoulli for horizontal pipe:** Setting $z_1 = z_2$: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate pressure drop:** $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Introduce the differential pressure head $h$:** By definition, $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$, so: $$h = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the continuity equation to express $v_1$ in terms of $v_2$:** From $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$, we obtain $v_1 = v_2 \cdot (A_2 / A_1)$. Substituting: $$h = \frac{v_2^2 - v_2^2 (A_2/A_1)^2}{2g} = \frac{v_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the throat velocity $v_2$:** $$v_2 = \sqrt{ \frac{2 g h}{1 - \left( A_2/A_1 \right)^2 } }$$ **Step 6 — Compute the theoretical volumetric flow rate:** $$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot v_2 = A_2 \sqrt{ \frac{2 g h}{1 - \left( A_2/A_1 \right)^2 } }$$ **Step 7 — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge $C_d$:** In real fluids, viscous effects and vena-contracta formation reduce the actual flow below the theoretical. The empirical correction factor is the coefficient of discharge $C_d$: $$\boxed{\; Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{ \frac{2 g h}{1 - \left( A_2/A_1 \right)^2 } } \;}$$ **Step 8 — Convert areas to diameters** (since pipe diameters $D_1, D_2$ are usually measured): Since $A = \pi D^2 / 4$: $$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left( \frac{D_2}{D_1} \right)^2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{D_2}{D_1} \right)^4$$ Therefore, the working discharge equation in **diameter form** is: $$\boxed{\; Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \left( D_2/D_1 \right)^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h} \;}$$ --- ### 3.3 Special-Case Derivations (Common in KTU Problems) **Case A — Pitot Tube (Single Point Stagnation):** A Pitot tube measures the **stagnation pressure** at a point where fluid velocity becomes zero ($v_2 = 0$). Applying Bernoulli between the free-stream point (1) and the stagnation point (2) along the same streamline: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + 0$$ $$\boxed{\; v_1 = \sqrt{ \frac{2 (P_2 - P_1)}{\rho} } = \sqrt{2 g h} \;}$$ This is the classic Pitot-tube velocity formula, where $h$ is the differential head. **Case B — Orifice Meter Approximation:** For an orifice plate, the throat area $A_2$ is much smaller than the pipe area $A_1$ (typically $A_2/A_1 < 0.1$). Therefore: $$1 - \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 \approx 1$$ This reduces the discharge equation to: $$Q_{act} \approx C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}$$ where $A_2$ is the orifice hole area. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) **Problem:** Water flows through a horizontal Venturi meter. The upstream pipe diameter is $D_1 = 0.20$ m, the throat diameter is $D_2 = 0.10$ m. The differential pressure head, measured by a U-tube manometer using mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³), is $h_m = 0.20$ m. If the coefficient of discharge is $C_d = 0.98$, calculate the actual volumetric flow rate. **Step 1 — Convert manometer reading to equivalent water head:** $$h = h_m \left( \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1 \right) = 0.20 \times \left( \frac{13600}{1000} - 1 \right)$$ $$h = 0.20 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.20 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \; m$$ **Step 2 — Compute area ratio squared:** $$\left( \frac{D_2}{D_1} \right)^4 = \left( \frac{0.10}{0.20} \right)^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$ **Step 3 — Compute the throat area:** $$A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$ **Step 4 — Apply the discharge equation:** $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$ $$Q_{act} = 0.98 \cdot \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52}$$ $$\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9683$$ $$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52} = \sqrt{49.44} = 7.031 \; m/s$$ $$Q_{act} = 0.98 \cdot \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9683} \cdot 7.031$$ $$Q_{act} = 0.98 \cdot 8.111 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 7.031$$ $$\boxed{\; Q_{act} \approx 0.0559 \; m^3/s \;=\; 55.9 \; L/s \;}$$ --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Bernoulli-Based Flow Measurement System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how Bernoulli's Equation is operationalized inside an industrial flow measurement loop, from physical phenomenon to final digital readout: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Process Pipe
Carrying Fluid] --> B[Primary Device
Venturi / Orifice / Nozzle / Pitot] B --> C[Pressure Taps
Upstream and Throat] C --> D[Differential Pressure
Transducer dP Cell] D --> E[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier and Filter] E --> F[ADC
Analog to Digital] F --> G[Microprocessor
Computes Q using Bernoulli Eq] G --> H[HMI Display
Flow Rate L per s] G --> I[Control Valve
Actuator Feedback] B -.-> J[Bernoulli Equation
P1 by rho g plus v1 sq by 2g
equals P2 by rho g plus v2 sq by 2g] J --> G ``` **Reading the diagram:** 1. The fluid flows through the **process pipe**. 2. The **primary device** (Venturi, orifice, nozzle, or Pitot) creates a pressure differential governed by Bernoulli's Equation. 3. **Pressure taps** sense the upstream and throat pressures. 4. The **dP transducer** converts the pressure difference into a low-level electrical signal (e.g., 4–20 mA). 5. The **signal conditioner** amplifies and filters the signal. 6. The **ADC** digitizes the signal for the microprocessor. 7. The **microprocessor** solves the Bernoulli-derived discharge equation in real time and outputs the flow rate. 8. The result is displayed on the **HMI** and may be used for **closed-loop control**. ### 4.2 Conceptual Energy-Transformation Diagram This Mermaid diagram shows how the **three forms of energy** in Bernoulli's Equation interchange as fluid moves through a constriction: ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Section 1: Wide Pipe
High Pressure Head P1 by rho g
Low Velocity Head v1 sq by 2g
Elevation z1] P2[Section 2: Throat
Low Pressure Head P2 by rho g
High Velocity Head v2 sq by 2g
Elevation z2] P1 -- Pressure converts to velocity --> P2 P2 -- Velocity converts to pressure --> P3[Section 3: Recovery
Pressure partially restored
Velocity decreases] P1 -. Same elevation horizontal pipe .-> P2 ``` ### 4.3 System-Level Comparison of Bernoulli-Based Flowmeters ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Bernoulli Based Flow Meters] --> M1[Venturi Meter
Cd equals 0.95 to 0.98
Lowest permanent pressure loss] Root --> M2[Orifice Plate
Cd equals 0.60 to 0.65
Cheapest and most common] Root --> M3[Flow Nozzle
Cd equals 0.93 to 0.98
Used for high velocity steam] Root --> M4[Pitot Static Tube
Cd equals 1.0 approx
Point velocity measurement] M1 --> APP1[Applications: Water treatment, Petrochemical] M2 --> APP2[Applications: Oil and gas, HVAC] M3 --> APP3[Applications: Power plants, Steam lines] M4 --> APP4[Applications: Aircraft, Wind tunnels] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023] > **State and explain Bernoulli's Equation. Mention the assumptions on which it is based.** **Model Answer:** Bernoulli's Equation, for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline between two points 1 and 2, is: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \text{constant}$$ It is derived from the principle of **conservation of mechanical energy**, expressing that the pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head sum to a constant. **Assumptions:** 1. Steady flow. 2. Incompressible fluid (constant $\rho$). 3. Inviscid (frictionless) flow. 4. Flow along a single streamline. 5. No energy addition or extraction between the two points. --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] > **What is a Venturi meter? Why is its coefficient of discharge close to unity?** **Model Answer:** A **Venturi meter** is a flow-measuring device consisting of a converging section (which accelerates the fluid), a narrow **throat**, and a gradually diverging section (which recovers most of the pressure). It is based on Bernoulli's Equation. The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ for a Venturi meter is very close to unity (typically **0.95 – 0.98**) because: - The smooth, gradual contraction and expansion **minimizes flow separation** and **turbulence**. - The **vena contracta** effect (which depresses $C_d$ in orifice plates) is negligible, because the throat is physically formed rather than allowed to form naturally. - The diverging cone (recovery cone) **recovers nearly all of the kinetic energy** as pressure, making energy losses very small. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **Q3. (A)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4] > **(a)** Derive Bernoulli's Equation from the principle of conservation of energy, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A horizontal orifice meter with a 50 mm diameter orifice is fitted in a 150 mm diameter pipe. The differential pressure across the orifice is measured as 80 kPa. If the coefficient of discharge is 0.62, calculate the volumetric flow rate of water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the exhaustive 8-step derivation. Key points to write in the answer script: - Define a fluid element moving between sections 1 and 2 along a streamline. - Compute work done by pressure forces: $W = (P_1 A_1 v_1 - P_2 A_2 v_2)\,dt$. - Apply continuity: $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$. - Equate work to change in KE + change in PE. - Simplify to obtain the standard Bernoulli form. **Model Solution for (b):** Given: $D_1 = 0.150$ m, $D_2 = 0.050$ m, $\Delta P = 80{,}000$ Pa, $C_d = 0.62$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. **Step 1 — Compute the differential pressure head:** $$h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{80{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 8.155 \; m$$ **Step 2 — Compute the area ratio term:** $$\left( \frac{D_2}{D_1} \right)^4 = \left( \frac{0.050}{0.150} \right)^4 = (0.3333)^4 = 0.01235$$ $$\sqrt{1 - 0.01235} = \sqrt{0.98765} = 0.9938$$ **Step 3 — Compute the orifice area:** $$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$ **Step 4 — Apply the discharge equation:** $$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$ $$Q = 0.62 \times \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9938} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 8.155}$$ $$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 8.155} = \sqrt{159.99} = 12.65 \; m/s$$ $$Q = 0.62 \times 1.9756 \times 10^{-3} \times 12.65$$ $$\boxed{\; Q \approx 1.55 \times 10^{-2} \; m^3/s \;=\; 15.5 \; L/s \;}$$ --- > **Q3. (B) — Alternative Choice for Internal Option** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] > **(a)** Explain the working principle of a Pitot-static tube with a neat sketch. Derive the velocity equation using Bernoulli's principle. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A Pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of air ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³) flowing in a duct. The differential manometer reading (using water, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³) is 10 mm. Find the air velocity. Take the Pitot coefficient $C_v = 0.98$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** A **Pitot-static tube** has two concentric tubes: - The **central (stagnation) tube** has its opening facing the flow, so it measures the **stagnation pressure** $P_2$ (where velocity drops to zero). - The **outer (static) tube** has its openings perpendicular to the flow, measuring the **static pressure** $P_1$ of the undisturbed stream. Applying Bernoulli's Equation between the free-stream point (1, velocity $v_1$, static pressure $P_1$) and the stagnation point (2, velocity $v_2 = 0$, stagnation pressure $P_2$): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + 0$$ $$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}}$$ Including the Pitot coefficient $C_v$: $$v_{act} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}}$$ **Model Solution for (b):** Given: $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $h_m = 0.010$ m, $C_v = 0.98$. **Step 1 — Convert the water-manometer reading to equivalent air-head:** $$h_{air} = h_m \left( \frac{\rho_w}{\rho_{air}} - 1 \right) \approx h_m \cdot \frac{\rho_w}{\rho_{air}} \;(\text{since } \rho_w \gg \rho_{air})$$ $$h_{air} = 0.010 \times \frac{1000}{1.225} = 8.163 \; m \text{ of air}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the velocity:** $$v = C_v \sqrt{2 g h_{air}} = 0.98 \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 8.163}$$ $$v = 0.98 \sqrt{160.16} = 0.98 \times 12.656$$ $$\boxed{\; v \approx 12.40 \; m/s \;}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert manometer reading to equivalent fluid head.** A U-tube manometer filled with mercury measures $h_m$ in cm of mercury, NOT in cm of water. The conversion $h = h_m(\rho_m/\rho_f - 1)$ is mandatory. > > 2. **Using $g = 10$ m/s² instead of 9.81 m/s².** While both may be accepted, using 9.81 is the **KTU standard** and yields more accurate results. > > 3. **Omitting the discharge coefficient $C_d$.** A theoretical answer (with $C_d = 1$) scores only partial credit. Always state and use $C_d$ explicitly. > > 4. **Confusing the Pitot-static equation with the Venturi equation.** Pitot tubes use a single stagnation point, so the term $1 - (A_2/A_1)^2$ is **absent**. > > 5. **Sign errors in elevation head.** For a downward-sloping pipe, $z_2 < z_1$. Students often write the wrong sign for the elevation difference. > > 6. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always quote $Q$ in m³/s or L/s, and $v$ in m/s. Board examiners explicitly deduct marks for missing units. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bernoulli's Equations (Module 4):** > > - Bernoulli's Equation is the **energy-conservation equation** for an ideal fluid: pressure head + velocity head + elevation head = constant. > - Assumptions: **steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no shaft work.** > - The equation is ALWAYS paired with the **continuity equation** $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ to solve real problems. > - For **real flows**, add a head-loss term $h_L$ (Darcy–Weisbach formula). > - **Venturi meter** — $C_d \approx 0.95$–$0.98$, lowest permanent pressure loss. > - **Orifice meter** — $C_d \approx 0.60$–$0.65$, cheapest and most common. > - **Pitot-static tube** — single-point velocity, no area ratio correction. > - Standard constant: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$; $\rho_{water} = \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3}$. > - The differential pressure head $h$ must be in **metres of the flowing fluid**, NOT metres of manometer fluid. > - Discharge equation (general): $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}} \sqrt{2 g h}$. > - $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ — coefficient of discharge is the product of contraction and velocity coefficients. > - Pitot-tube velocity: $v = C_v \sqrt{2 g h}$ (no area correction needed). > - Always carry units through every calculation and quote final answers with correct SI units. > - When the pipe is **horizontal**, the elevation head cancels out — a common simplification in KTU problems. ---
